discussionPrompt.title: Week 8 - AI Tools and Human Memory: Offloading or Augmenting Cognition?
discussionPrompt.body: A minimum of 2 posts per week is required. Your initial post should: (1) draw on at least one concept from this week's readings, (2) interpret how it applies to your own experience with AI tools, and (3) connect your thinking to a classmate's post in your reply. Please post by Thursday midnight and reply by Saturday 9am.

posts.text: I use ChatGPT a lot for summarizing readings and it definitely saves time. The cognitive offloading concept from the readings is relevant here—I think I'm offloading memory tasks to the AI. It's convenient but I sometimes wonder if I'm actually learning less. The readings were interesting this week.

I connected this week's topic to how I use Google Maps. I never memorize routes anymore because I just rely on the app. This seems like cognitive offloading too. I think AI is similar—we rely on it instead of building our own knowledge. It's a habit I've noticed in myself.

AI writing tools feel like a double-edged sword. They help me get started when I'm stuck, but I'm not sure how much I'm actually developing my own writing skills. The readings talked about extended cognition which I thought was interesting. I think there's a balance to find.

I agree that cognitive offloading is happening with AI. I use Notion AI to organize my notes and it does a lot of the structuring for me. I used to do that myself. I think this is related to what the readings said about transactive memory. Good topic this week.

I think AI can actually help with metacognition if used correctly. When I ask ChatGPT to explain my reasoning back to me, it helps me see gaps. But I know most people don't use it that way. I found this week's readings on metacognitive regulation useful.
Stella, I had the same thought about Google Maps. I also can't remember phone numbers anymore. I think we're both saying the same thing about offloading. Good point.
Liz, I think you're right that there's a balance. AI tools are helpful but we need to make sure we're still learning. I've noticed the same thing with writing tools.
Addison, the Notion AI point is interesting. I use similar tools. I think we are all saying that AI is changing how we organize and remember things. It's something to think about.
Matty, that's a good point about metacognition. I haven't thought about using AI that way. Maybe I should try it.
Jennifer, I think your point about learning less is important. That's what worries me too. We should be intentional about when we use AI.

clusters.label:
Cognitive Offloading
& AI Dependence
clusters.consensusWarning: 6 posts converge on the same concern about AI dependence. No one has challenged this view or offered a counter-perspective.
